title: Prenatal Care and a Healthy Pregnancy: First-Visit Teaching
subtitle: A healthy 27-year-old at about 10 weeks comes in for her first prenatal visit. The nurse teaches folic acid, nutrition, vaccines, substances and foods to avoid, and the danger signs that need prompt reporting.

patient.name: Hana Okada
patient.admitDate: Prenatal clinic, first visit at ~10 weeks (G1). Healthy with a normal BMI and no chronic conditions; mild first-trimester nausea. Here for initial prenatal assessment, screening, and health teaching.

vitals.time: If a danger sign is ignored (compare)
vitals.temp: Severe headache, visual changes, edema — preeclampsia missed

nursesNote: Hana Okada, 27, G1 at ~10 weeks, healthy, here for her first prenatal visit. Teaching: take a daily prenatal vitamin with folic acid (prevents neural tube defects); eat a balanced diet with adequate protein/iron/calcium and appropriate weight gain; get the inactivated influenza vaccine and Tdap while AVOIDING live vaccines (MMR/varicella); avoid alcohol (no safe amount), tobacco, and listeria foods (unpasteurized dairy, deli meats unless heated, raw/undercooked items); avoid cat litter (toxoplasmosis); exercise moderately. Report danger signs promptly — vaginal bleeding, severe/persistent headache, visual changes, facial/hand edema (preeclampsia), severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fever, leaking fluid, or decreased fetal movement.
providerOrders: Confirm pregnancy and dating; complete the initial prenatal history and exam
Order initial prenatal labs/screening; schedule routine visits
Prescribe a prenatal vitamin with folic acid; review nutrition and weight gain
Recommend the inactivated influenza vaccine and Tdap; avoid live vaccines
Teach danger signs, substances/foods to avoid, and when to seek care

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does early prenatal care include?

opts.text: Folic acid before and early in pregnancy helps prevent neural tube defects
Routine prenatal care includes scheduled visits, screening, and teaching
Danger signs like bleeding, severe headache, or visual changes need prompt reporting
The inactivated flu vaccine and Tdap are recommended in pregnancy
A small amount of alcohol is safe during pregnancy
Food safety matters — avoid foods that can carry listeria
There is no known safe amount of alcohol in pregnancy
Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) are avoided during pregnancy
Changing cat litter risks toxoplasmosis and should be avoided
Moderate exercise is generally encouraged in an uncomplicated pregnancy
All medications are safe to continue without review in pregnancy
Severe headache, visual changes, and facial edema may signal preeclampsia
Take a thorough history and confirm/date the pregnancy
Order initial prenatal labs and screening
Start a prenatal vitamin with folic acid and review nutrition
Teach danger signs, safe behaviors, and what to avoid
Schedule follow-up visits and provide contact resources; document
Take a daily prenatal vitamin with folic acid
Get the inactivated influenza vaccine and Tdap
Eat a balanced diet with adequate protein, iron, and calcium
Do moderate, regular exercise as tolerated
Report bleeding, severe headache, or visual changes promptly
Drink alcohol "in moderation"
Get the live MMR or varicella vaccine now
Change the cat's litter box daily
Eat unpasteurized cheese and deli meats

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Review the health history and current medications for safety
Reinforce folic acid, nutrition, and recommended vaccines
Teach what to avoid (alcohol, tobacco, listeria foods, cat litter, live vaccines)
Teach danger signs and when to call or seek care
Confirm the visit schedule and follow-up; document teaching

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.sub: Expected finding or a danger sign to report?

opts.text: Mild nausea in the first trimester
Mild fatigue and breast tenderness
Occasional mild lower-back ache
Feeling fetal movement (quickening) in the second trimester
Vaginal bleeding
Severe headache with visual changes and facial swelling
Persistent vomiting with inability to keep fluids down
A gush of fluid or markedly decreased fetal movement
